text.title: reranking-pipeline-integration Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for reranking-pipeline-integration. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: When using the Cohere Rerank API, always map results back to original documents using r.index, never by list position. Cohere returns results in score order, not input order.
rule.rationale: Cohere's response.results is sorted by relevance score descending. Using list position instead of r.index maps rerank scores to the wrong documents, silently corrupting the ranking.

rule.statement: Every production reranking call MUST have a fallback path that returns initial retrieval results when the reranker fails, times out, or is unavailable. Never let a reranker failure propagate as a query failure.
rule.rationale: The reranker is an accuracy improvement, not a correctness requirement. Initial retrieval results are useful; zero results or an exception is not. Failing silently to initial order preserves user experience.

rule.statement: Cache reranked results by hash(query + sorted(doc_ids)). Identical queries with the same candidate pool recur frequently in chat interfaces where the same context window is reused across turns.
rule.rationale: Reranking is computationally expensive (100-500ms). Caching avoids redundant scoring when the same query is issued repeatedly within a session or across concurrent users asking similar questions.

rule.statement: LLM-based reranking prompts MUST constrain output to JSON with a defined schema. Without schema enforcement the agent may produce freeform ranking prose that breaks the downstream parser.
rule.rationale: LLMs produce variable-format output when unconstrained. A ranking expressed as natural language prose ("the first document seems most relevant...") cannot be parsed into a sorted index. JSON with explicit index and score fields is parseable deterministically.

rule.statement: For multilingual content, use Cohere rerank-multilingual-v3.0 or Jina jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual. Never use an English-only model (ms-marco family) on non-English content.
rule.rationale: English-only models silently produce degraded scores on non-English text. The multilingual variants cover 100+ languages and maintain precision across language boundaries.

rule.statement: On CPU-only infrastructure, use FlashRank instead of sentence-transformers CrossEncoder. FlashRank is designed for CPU inference and delivers 3-5x faster scoring without requiring GPU.
rule.rationale: sentence-transformers CrossEncoder defaults to CPU but is not optimized for it. FlashRank uses quantized models and CPU-optimized inference paths, making local reranking viable on commodity servers.
